title: MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
text:
26 January 2026 01:37 PM
In the name of
His Highness Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
President of the United Arab Emirates/ Ruler of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE
COMMERCIAL AND CIVIL DIVISION
BETWEEN
MEDIAMAM HOLDINGS LIMITED
Claimant
and
BEYOND DREAMS FOR GENERAL TRADING WLL
Defendant
JUDGMENT OF JUSTICE PAUL HEATH KC

26 January 2026 01:37 PM
Neutral Citation: [2026] ADGMCFI 0003
Before: Justice Paul Heath KC
Decision Date: 26 January 2026
Decision: Orders for alternative service granted under rule 19(1)
Hearing Date(s): On the papers
Date of Orders: 26 January 2026
Catchwords: Service of process under a multilateral treaty.
Application for alternative service. Whether “exceptional
circumstances” exist to depart from treaty service.
Legislation Cited: ADGM Court Procedure Rules 2016
1996 Gulf Cooperation Council Convention for the
Execution of Judgments, Delegations and Judicial
Notifications
Cases Cited: Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC v Shetty [2021]
ADGMCFI 0004
NMC Healthcare Ltd (In Administration) v Shetty [2023]
ADGMCFI 0014
Case Number: ADGMCFI-2025-208
Parties and representation: Ahmad Ahmad of Salt and Associates FZ-LLC for the
Claimant
JUDGMENT
The Application
1. On 25 June 2025, MediAmAm Holdings Ltd (“MediAmAm”) issued a Claim against Beyond
Dreams for General Trading WLL (“Beyond Dreams”) by which it seeks judgment in the
sum of USD 1,300,000, together with interest and costs. Because Beyond Dreams is a
company registered in Kuwait, service must be effected outside the jurisdiction of the Abu
Dhabi Global Market (“ADGM”).
2. By rule 28(3) of the ADGM Court Procedure Rules 2016 (the “Rules”), service was to be
effected within six months of the date of issue of the claim form. For reasons into which I
go later, service within that time could not be achieved. On 20 November 2025, under
rule 28(4) of the Rules, I made an order extending the time for service of the Claim until 4
April 2026 (the “November Order”).
3. Service has not yet been achieved. On 14 January 2026, MediAmAm applied to serve the
Claim by alternative means, in reliance on rule 19 of the Rules (the “Service Application”).
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-208 - MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
1

26 January 2026 01:37 PM
As Beyond Dreams has not been served with the Claim, the Service Application is made on
a without notice basis.
4. Relevantly, rule 19 of the Rules states:
“19. Service of documents by an alternative method or at an
alternative place
(1) Where it appears to the Court that there is a good reason to authorise
service of the claim form or any other document by a method or at a place
not otherwise permitted by this Part, the Court may make an order
permitting service by an alternative method or at an alternative place.
(2) On an application under this Rule, the Court may order that steps already
taken to bring the claim form or any other document to a person’s
attention by an alternative method or at an alternative place is effective
service.
(3) An application under this Rule must be supported by evidence, and may
be made without notice to the other party.”
Background
5. The United Arab Emirates (“UAE”) of which, for this purpose ADGM is a part, and Kuwait
are parties to the 1996 Gulf Cooperation Council Convention for the Execution of
Judgments, Delegations and Judicial Notifications (the “GCC Convention”). Article 20 of
the GCC Convention states that judicial documents requested to be served in any Member
State “shall be sent directly from the competent judicial authority or employee to the
competent Court or authority in which jurisdiction the person required to be notified
resides, in accordance with the laws of such court or authority”.
6. MediAmAm has taken steps in an endeavour to serve Beyond Dreams in Kuwait under the
GCC Convention. MediAmAm arranged for the Claim and its supporting documents (the
“Claim Documents”) to be translated into Arabic and attested by the Ministry of Justice of
the UAE on 8 August 2025. Subsequently, on 1 September 2025, the Claim Documents
were attested by the UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Embassy of Kuwait in Abu
Dhabi. Following those processes, the Claim Documents were sent, on 9 September 2025,
by courier, to counsel instructed in Kuwait. The Kuwait Ministry of Foreign Affairs attested
the documents on 14 October 2025. However, the Kuwait Ministry of Justice declined to
legalise the Claim Documents, insisting on obtaining “original hard copies”.
7. No wet-ink or physical original of the Claim Documents exists, as claim forms before
ADGM Courts are issued exclusively in digital form. Under rule 13(1) of the Rules, claim
forms and all other documents are filed via the ADGM Courts’ electronic filing and case
management system, known as the eCourts Platform.1
1 ADGM Courts’ Practice Direction 1.10.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-208 - MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
2

26 January 2026 01:37 PM
8. Notwithstanding explanations about the digital operating system used by the ADGM Court,
a final attempt to obtain attestation from the Kuwait Ministry of Justice was rejected on 26
October 2025. That process was completed before the November Order, which extended
the time for service until 24 April 2026.
9. The Service Application is supported by a witness statement of Mr Ahmad Ahmad,
MediAmAm’s legal representative in ADGM. Supporting evidence has been obtained from
Kuwaiti counsel instructed by MediAmAm, Mr Khalifa Al Yaqout. I accept that Mr Al Yaqout
is qualified to give expert evidence on Kuwaiti law. His evidence explains the means by
which domestic and foreign processes may be served in Kuwait under Kuwaiti law.
10. My attention has also been drawn to two decisions given by Justice Sir Andrew Smith,2
sitting in this Court, in which the Judge made comments about whether this Court should
exercise jurisdiction under rule 19 in circumstances where the jurisdictions in which the
claim has been filed (on the one hand) and is to be served (on the other) are different and
each State is a party to an international agreement dealing with the question of service.
Analysis
11. I accept that, as the UAE and Kuwait are both parties to the GCC Convention, its terms set
out the primary mode by which service of a process in one of the Member State’s Courts is
to be completed in the jurisdiction of another Member State.
12. The question is whether other valid methods of effecting service under Kuwaiti law can be
used in circumstances where the Kuwaiti Ministry of Justice declines to implement the
service process under the terms of the GCC Convention. The expert evidence from Mr Al
Yaqout satisfies me that the GCC Convention is not, as a matter of Kuwaiti law, the sole
means by which service of a foreign process can be effected in Kuwait. Mr Al Yaqout’s
evidence is that service of foreign process in Kuwait can follow the “usual means
recognized by the CCPL [Kuwaiti Decree Law No 38/1980]”, which are said to include
courier delivery and registered email of the claim form.
13. This Court’s jurisdiction to make an order under rule 19 in circumstances where an
international agreement governs the mode of service was considered in Abu Dhabi
Commercial Bank PJSC v Shetty3 and NMC Healthcare Ltd (In Administration) v Shetty.4
Those cases involved the need for service on residents of the Republic of India in
circumstances where a bilateral treaty existed between the Republic of India and UAE
dealing with the question of service: see the Juridical and Judicial Cooperation in Civil and
Commercial Matters for the Service of Summons, Judicial Documents, Commissions,
Execution of Judgments and Arbitral Awards of 25 October 1999.
14. In Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC, Justice Sir Andrew Smith held that “where there is a
relevant bilateral treaty, the court will make an order [under rule 19] only exceptionally”.5
2 Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC v Shetty [2021] ADGMCFI 0004 and NMC Healthcare Ltd (In Administration) v Shetty [2023]
ADGMCFI 0014. See also paragraphs 13 and 14 below.
3 Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC v Shetty [2021] ADGMCFI 0004, at paragraph 14.
4 NMC Healthcare Ltd (In Administration) v Shetty [2023] ADGMCFI 0014, at paragraphs 7-14.
5 Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC v Shetty [2021] ADGMCFI 0004, at paragraph 14.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-208 - MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
3

26 January 2026 01:37 PM
In NMC Healthcare, after reviewing a decision of Marcus Smith J in English proceedings
involving NMC Healthcare, Justice Sir Andrew Smith decided to grant the application under
rule 19(2) of the Rules, which His Honour found to be the appropriate provision for this
purpose.6 The Judge said:7
“10. I come to the applications before me under the CPR r.19(2). For reasons
that I need not explain and are apparent from Mr Marsh's evidence, it is
overwhelmingly likely that Dr Shetty and Mr Manghat are well aware of
these proceedings. Further, it is clear that service of the proceedings of
them in India would involve additional cost and would cause delay.
However, I observed in my judgment in ADCB v Shetty (cit sup) that, on an
application under CPR r. 19(1) for the permission for service by an
alternative method in circumstances where there is a relevant bilateral
treaty, delay and costs are not sufficient reason for an order and, as I have
said, the Court will make an order only exceptionally. I observe that the
CPR require a good reason for permitting service by an alternative method,
and that there is no express requirement to that effect in CPR r. 19(2). That
said, to my mind similar considerations arise under the two CPR r. 19
provisions where there is a bilateral treaty, and I proceed on the basis that
the Court should only exceptionally accede to an application under CPR
r.19(2) in such circumstances.”
15. The only issue on the Service Application is whether the term “exceptionally”, as used by
Justice Sir Andrew Smith does or does not permit the making of an order on the present
facts. In my view, the definition of the term “exceptionally” is not set in concrete. Use of
the term in a judgment should not be interpreted as if it were a statutory test. Rather, the
approach identified in Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC enables the Court to consider
whether an order is justified on the particular facts before it. To ensure appropriate
application of the term, the Court starts from the default position that an order under rule
19(1) is regarded as the exception rather than the rule. Some circumstances taking the
case outside of the standard procedure must be found in order to exercise what is regarded
as an “exceptional” jurisdiction.
16. On the basis of Mr Al Yaqout’s evidence, I am satisfied that Kuwaiti law authorises the
service of a foreign process (such as one originating in ADGM) by courier and email in
Kuwait and does not regard the GCC Convention as providing the sole basis for the service
of Court documents from a Member State. Accordingly, there is no reason why this Court
should not exercise its discretion under rule 19(1) to direct service by an alternative means.
Result
17. For those reasons, I make the following orders:
6 Rule 19(2) is set out at paragraph 4 above.
7 NMC Healthcare Ltd (In Administration) v Shetty [2023] ADGMCFI 0014, at paragraph 10.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-208 - MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
4

26 January 2026 01:37 PM
a. MediAmAm shall serve the Claim Documents, the November Order, the Service
Application, and the order giving effect to this judgment within 21 days of the date of
this judgment by both:
i. Sending these by email to Mr Abdullah Boushahri, a director of Beyond
Dreams; and
ii. Delivering these by courier to the offices of Beyond Dreams.
b. MediAmAm shall file Certificates of Service in respect of each method of alternative
service.
c. Beyond Dreams shall have a further 14 days within which to file an
Acknowledgement of Service, and to file any application (if so advised) to set aside
or vary the Order that is the subject of this judgment.
d. If no application is filed to set aside or vary, Beyond Dreams is to file its response to
the Claim within a further period of 14 days.
e. Costs are reserved.
f. General liberty to apply.
Issued by:
Linda Fitz-Alan
Registrar, ADGM Courts
26 January 2026
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-208 - MediAmAm Holdings Limited v. Beyond Dreams for General Trading WLL
5
